title: The Lord is My Light
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Lord is My Light - Pastor Tito Lyro - Psalm 27.txt
outline: The Lord is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear? (v.1)
When evildoers assail; in this I am confident (vv.2-3)
One thing I ask: to dwell in the house of the Lord (v.4)
He will hide me in the day of trouble (v.5)
Do not hide Your face from me (v.9)
Seek His face — my heart says seek Your face (v.8)
Wait on the Lord, be of good courage (v.14)
summary: An AM sermon on Psalm 27, the psalm of confident trust in God as light, salvation, and stronghold. Despite enemies and threats, the psalmist's singular desire is to dwell in God's presence and seek His face. The sermon explores how this confidence is not naivety but faith rooted in God's character, and how waiting on the Lord sustains the believer through every trial.
transcript:
There is no way of knowing exactly when this Psalm was written, or what the particular occasion was. All the title tells us is that this is a Psalm of David — a song of David. The content of the Psalm probably describes David's life not at one particular moment, but in general as he lived it. It is clear that David was pursued by enemies, cut off from the house of the Lord, and subject to slander — things that happened throughout his whole life. We don't necessarily have to identify a specific occasion in order to understand what is going on, because this Psalm is characteristic of his whole life.

Whatever the circumstances were, David addresses things that all of us have gone through or are going through. Have you ever dealt with fear, or had enemies? Have you cried out to God for deliverance? Have you been forsaken by close family because of the gospel of Jesus Christ? Have you ever been accused of something you didn't do? Have people who were close to you ever lied about you? Have you ever sinned and been in need of forgiveness? If you can say yes to any of these questions, this Psalm should speak to your heart.

We can see at least four things David is thinking about in this Psalm: his delight in the Lord, the deliverance the Lord brought to him, his desire for the Lord, and his dependence on the Lord. These four concepts are intertwined throughout the Psalm. We are going to try to separate them as we consider Psalm 27 this morning.

## David's Delight in the Lord (vv. 1–3)

Why did David delight in the Lord? He delighted in the Lord because of his confidence in him.

> *"The Lord is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? The Lord is the stronghold of my life; of whom shall I be afraid?"* (Psalm 27:1)

Look at verse 3: *"Though an army encamp against me, my heart shall not fear; though war arise against me, yet I will be confident."* David is saying: no matter what happens, I will continue to trust the Lord.

David's confidence is based on God's providence and God's Word. In verse 2, he speaks of God's providence: *"When evildoers assail me to eat up my flesh, my adversaries and foes, it is they who stumble and fall."* David had experienced God's deliverance — from Saul, from Absalom, in the fulfillment of God's promise to make him king. The providence of God gave David reasons to be confident.

Even more than that, God's Word gave him confidence. David chooses a specific word for the Lord in verse 1 — God's covenantal name, Yahweh. This is how God revealed himself to his people. David embraces that concept and calls him the God who is ever-present: *I AM*, Yahweh, God's personal name. Remember when Moses stood before the burning bush: God said, *"I AM WHO I AM."* Tell them that is my name. And by that name they will know who I am. David's knowledge of God's Word gave him confidence that his God would be faithful, because his God is the God who made covenant with his people.

David knew about God's faithfulness to him *despite* his own unfaithfulness to God. Praise the Lord that we have a God who is faithful to us even when we are unfaithful to him.

Turn with me to Psalm 103. Look at verses 1–5:

> *"Bless the Lord, O my soul, and all that is within me, bless his holy name! Bless the Lord, O my soul, and forget not all his benefits — who forgives all your iniquity, who heals all your diseases, who redeems your life from the pit, who crowns you with steadfast love and mercy, who satisfies you with good so that your youth is renewed like the eagle's."*

What is David doing here? He is preaching to himself. Perhaps he is in a moment of depression or despair or weak faith. So what does he do? He turns to what he knows to be true concerning God — from providence and from the Word — and he says: *Bless the Lord. Be happy in the Lord.* That is what the word "blessed" means — to be happy. Be happy in the Lord! Why? Because of his benefits. Don't forget all his benefits.

People of God, we can be confident in our God because he has redeemed us, and he is faithful even when we are unfaithful. We need to preach that to ourselves. There is nothing in us that caused God to redeem us. It is completely because of his grace. Not one ounce of goodness in us caused God to say, "I am going to redeem that person." It is solely because of the pleasure of his good will that he decided to save you, to save me. And because he is faithful to himself, he will never abandon us.

Look at Psalm 103:13–14:

> *"As a father shows compassion to his children, so the Lord shows compassion to those who fear him. For he knows our frame; he remembers that we are dust."*

God knows what we are made of. He knows what we can bear. He condescends to us in our frailty. He is faithful to us and he pities us as a loving father pities his children. And because of that, David can delight in him.

Look at Psalm 103:17–18:

> *"But the steadfast love of the Lord is from everlasting to everlasting on those who fear him, and his righteousness to children's children, to those who keep his covenant and remember to do his commandments."*

To fear God is to believe in him. At the core of it, to fear God is to believe in the Lord Jesus Christ — to believe that you deserve hell forever, that there is nothing in you that should cause God to have compassion on you, that the just consequence for your existence is eternal separation from God, bearing his wrath. But to fear God is also to believe that the Lord Jesus Christ became human like you and me, obeyed God perfectly — the obedience that you and I should have given — and died bearing the wrath of God for your sin. And that God raised him from the dead in recognition that his life and death were accepted. To fear God is to believe that that life and that cross are yours. To have the righteousness of Christ counted as yours. To have the guilt of your sins counted as Christ's. That is what it means to fear God: not to trust in anything in yourself, but in Christ alone.

And there in verse 17, the mercy of the Lord is from everlasting to everlasting. You cannot travel so far into the past or so far into the future that you would find a time when the mercy of God is absent toward those who fear him. David preaches that to himself, and because of that he can delight in the Lord.

We can have the same confidence. The things God has done in our lives show his faithfulness to us. We all have providential evidences. But we tend to remember only the last thing that happened — and if that last thing wasn't what we liked, we think that is how God always deals with us. Recency bias. We use today to determine God's love: good things happened, so God loves me; hard things happened, so God doesn't love me. That is not how we know the love of God. We know the love of God because he has saved us in Jesus Christ. If Christ is your Savior today, then delight in the Lord — for he loves you.

Paul writes in 2 Timothy 2:

> *"Therefore I endure everything for the sake of the elect, that they also may obtain the salvation that is in Christ Jesus with eternal glory... This is a faithful saying: if we died with him, we shall also live with him; if we endure, we shall also reign with him; if we are faithless, he remains faithful — he cannot deny himself."*

You may be struggling with your faith even now. You may be in despair. You may be at the bottom of depression, not thinking there is one ounce of faith or grace in you. Even now, he is faithful to you. He will never leave you nor forsake you. It is through these rivers that he is carrying you. It is through these fires that he is carrying you through, even at your lowest.

Father, when you look at a believer in Jesus Christ, you see the face of your Son. You say, *I love him, for when I look at him, I see my Son pleading for him before me.* The Bible tells us that God has entered into a covenant to give us a new heart and to be forever faithful to us. That new covenant was fulfilled in the Lord Jesus Christ. Because of that, we can have confidence that our Lord will be faithful. And in that confidence, we can delight in the God in whom David delighted.

> *"The Lord is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear?"*

Light implies security, comfort, guidance, courage. But ultimately, the Lord is our light in Jesus Christ. Our Savior says: *"I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life"* (John 8:12). That is you, Christian. Christ is your light.

God is David's salvation (v. 1). David is probably referring to physical deliverance from enemies — but the confidence that God is our salvation is found throughout Scripture. We are not saved because of ourselves but *despite* ourselves. And because we are saved despite ourselves, there is nothing we can do to separate us from God. And God is David's strength. We can turn to God for the strength needed to go through life. Whatever God calls us to do, he is going to strengthen us to do it — at the moment we need it. But we have to wait. We have to depend on him for his strength. *"They who wait for the Lord shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings like eagles"* (Isaiah 40:31). Those who are weak are going to soar because they are waiting on the Lord.

## David's Delight in God's Deliverance (vv. 4–6)

David knows that deliverance comes from God.

> *"For he will hide me in his shelter in the day of trouble; he will conceal me under the cover of his tent; he will lift me high upon a rock."* (Psalm 27:5)

One of my favorite Psalms is Psalm 40, where I believe David is recounting the experience of being redeemed by the Lord. He describes the miry pit — the bottom of a dry well, all mud, like sinking sand. You are there, stuck, the more you move the more you sink, it is dark, there is no hope, you are screaming and nobody hears you. And he says: *I was in the miry pit — yet the Lord reached down, pulled me out, set me upon a rock, and gave me a new song.* That is what the Lord does for us when he redeems us.

That rock is the Lord Jesus Christ — the firm foundation of our salvation in the time of trouble. God delivers his people. And sometimes he delivers them *through* the problem, not around it. Remember what Isaiah says: *"When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; and through the rivers, they shall not overwhelm you; when you walk through fire you shall not be burned."* It doesn't say: I will remove you from the waters. It says: when you are going through them, I am going with you. Sometimes the Lord delivers us from suffering *through* suffering.

Like a mother hen hiding her chicks under her wings, God hides his people:

> *"He will cover you with his pinions, and under his wings you will find refuge; his faithfulness is a shield and buckler."* (Psalm 91:4)

> *"How precious is your steadfast love, O God! The children of mankind take refuge in the shadow of your wings."* (Psalm 36:7)

When the bird of prey flies over, the mother hen calls, and all the chicks run under her wings. She protects them by giving of herself. Isn't that what the Lord Jesus Christ has done for us? Not under his wings necessarily, but under the shadow of the cross. When the angel of death comes upon us, what does he see? He sees the blood of Christ, and he passes over. We are delivered.

In Psalm 32:7, David says: *"You are a hiding place for me; you preserve me from trouble; you surround me with shouts of deliverance."*

Knowing that God delivers us from our troubles must propel us to serving him — to action. God calls us, as James says, to be hearers *and* doers of his Word. He is after the heart and after the hands. We are to believe and to do as a result of that belief.

## David's Desire for the Lord (vv. 4, 6)

> *"One thing have I asked of the Lord, that will I seek after: that I may dwell in the house of the Lord all the days of my life, to gaze upon the beauty of the Lord and to inquire in his temple."* (Psalm 27:4)

By saying that *one thing* he desires, David is saying this is the thing he desires most. Compared to anything else, this desire is so great that other desires seem not to exist. But he doesn't just rest there with the desire. He puts it into practice: *this is what I desire, and this is what I am going to seek.* He will seek to dwell in the house of the Lord in order to behold the beauty of the Lord and to inquire of him.

To seek to dwell in the house of the Lord is to seek to live in the presence of the Lord as represented by the weekly worship of God's people. Because we know that God can deliver us from whatever temptation we face, we must earnestly seek to live in his beautiful presence.

Is God beautiful to you? Have you seen him through Jesus Christ? If you look at God apart from Jesus Christ, the only thing you can do is tremble — because apart from grace, the only thing God has for you is wrath. But through Jesus Christ, he is a delivering God — a God who has changed your heart, given you his Spirit, and is transforming you to be more like the Lord Jesus Christ.

The Psalmists and the prophets often speak of God as beautiful, using the phrase *the beauty of his holiness* — another way of saying the beauty of his being *God*, utterly other than us. Our God is infinite, eternal, unchangeable in all his characteristics. He is not just us but a little bigger, a little better, a little stronger. He is completely other than us. And that is a beautiful thing.

David knew that God had delivered him *through* suffering. When Saul persecuted him and he had to live in the deserts and caves — that was not fun. When Absalom came into Jerusalem and he had to flee — that was not fun. And yet he talks of those times as the deliverances of the Lord. It was in those times that he saw the Lord the most clearly. Psalms 62 and 63 were written in those moments, and in them he says: I just want to see the beauty of the Lord.

His faith in God translates into action. In verse 6 he says: *"And now my head shall be lifted up above my enemies all around me, and I will offer in his tent sacrifices with shouts of joy; I will sing and make melody to the Lord."* Because of who God is, I am going to worship him — and worship him wholeheartedly.

## Wait on the Lord (v. 14)

May our delight be in this God — the God of David, the God of his church, the God who revealed himself to us through the Lord Jesus Christ. May we live according to the truth that deliverance comes from the Lord through the Lord Jesus Christ. May we desire and depend on him.

> *"Wait for the Lord; be strong, and let your heart take courage; wait for the Lord!"* (Psalm 27:14)

That is: depend on him. Be of good courage. He shall strengthen your heart. Wait on the Lord.
